1: wᵉʾēlleh habbāʾîm ʾel-dāwid bᵉṣiqlag — ‘and these are those who came to David at Ziklag.’ Ziklag was the Philistine city given to David during his exile (1 Samuel 27:6). bᵉʿôḏ ʿāṣûr mippᵉnê šāʾûl ben-qîš — ‘while still restrained from before Saul son of Kish’ — the fugitive period. The Chronicler includes this section to show that David’s all-Israel coalition began forming before he was king, predating any political opportunity.
2: middōšᵉqê qešet — ‘armed with the bow, right-handed and left-handed in hurling stones.’ The Benjaminite warriors’ ambidexterity is a military specialty noted also in Judges 20:16 (the men of Gibeah who could sling a stone to a hair’s breadth). Their joining David from Saul’s own tribe is particularly significant — Benjamin loyalty shifting from Saul to David.
3: The list of Benjaminite warriors begins with Ahiezer and Joash sons of Shemaah the Gibeathite. Gibeah, again, is Saul’s city. These Benjaminites from Saul’s hometown came to David, the ultimate statement of cross-tribal loyalty. Jeziel and Pelet sons of Azmaveth — Azmaveth also appears in the Mighty Men list (1 Chronicles 11:33).
4: Ishmaiah the Gibeonite is noted as ‘mighty among the Thirty and over the Thirty’ — i.e., ranking within the elite corps already established. Jeremiah, Jahaziel, Johanan, and Josabad the Gederathite fill out the contingent. The overlapping names with the Mighty Men list suggest the two lists derive from the same military administrative source.
5: Eluzai, Jerimoth, Bealiah, Shemariah, and Shephatiah the Haruphite. The Haruph clan is mentioned in Nehemiah 7:24 as returnees from exile — linking these pre-exile warriors to the post-exile community the Chronicler is addressing.
6: Elkanah, Isshiah, Azarel, Joezer, and Jashobeam the Korahites. The Korahites were a Levitical guild associated with temple music and gatekeeping (1 Chronicles 6:22; 1 Chronicles 9:19). Their appearance in a warrior list suggests the boundary between cultic and military roles was permeable in the early monarchy.
7: Joelah and Zebadiah sons of Jeroham from Gedor. Gedor is a Judahite town (1 Chronicles 4:4) — a further note of Judahite support for David supplementing the Benjaminite contingent.
8: ûmin-haggāḏî niphrᵉḏû ʾel-dāwid lamlᵉḏbārāh — ‘from the Gadites there separated themselves to David in the wilderness.’ The desert/wilderness period references David’s time in the Judean wilderness (1 Sam 22-26). These Gadites, from trans-Jordan, crossed to join the fugitive king. gibborê ḥayil anšê ṣāḇāʾ lamilḥāmāh maʿarᵉḵê ṣinnāh warōmaḥ — ‘valiant men, warriors for war, trained to handle shield and spear.’ ûp̄ᵉnêhem pᵉnê ʾaryēh — ‘their faces were like the faces of lions’ — the lion-face simile for fierce warriors.
9: The Gadite commanders are listed in order: Ezer the first, Obadiah the second, Eliab the third. The ordinal numbering (hārîʾšônāh, haššᵉnî, haššᵉlîšî, etc.) continues through 1 Chronicles 12:13, imposing a clear hierarchical structure. This military ranking system shows sophisticated administrative organization during David’s fugitive period.
10: Mishmannah the fourth, Jeremiah the fifth — the list continues. The Gadite names recur in the trans-Jordanian genealogies of 1 Chronicles 5. The Chronicler links these warriors back to their tribal genealogies, showing the all-Israel framework of the David narrative.
11: Attai the sixth, Eliel the seventh. The name Eliel appears multiple times in these lists (1 Chronicles 11:46-47; 1 Chronicles 12:11) — likely different individuals sharing a common theophoric name (‘my God is God’).
12: Johanan the eighth, Elzabad the ninth. The list of Gadite officers reaches its tenth at 1 Chronicles 12:13. The eleven-man named unit reflects a military decimal structure (cf. the tribal warrior counts in 1 Chronicles 7 and 1 Chronicles 12:23-37).
13: Jeremiah the tenth, Machbannai the eleventh. The complete Gadite officer list has eleven men. Verse 14’s summary notes they ranged from commanders of a hundred to commanders of a thousand, showing these were mid-level officers, not common soldiers.
14: min-bᵉnê-gāḏ rāʾšê haṣṣāḇāʾ — ‘from the sons of Gad, commanders of the army.’ The superlative: haqqāṭōn lᵉmeʾāh wᵉhaggāḏôl laʾelep̄ — ‘the least over a hundred and the greatest over a thousand.’ This telescoping of magnitude is a rhetorical device emphasizing the quality of the entire contingent.
15: hēmmāh ʿāḇrû ʾet-hayyardēn baḥōḏeš hārîʾšôn — ‘they crossed the Jordan in the first month’ — during spring flooding (kî mālaʾ ʿal kol-gᵉḏôtāyw — ‘when it had overflowed all its banks’). Crossing a flood-stage Jordan to join David was an act of extraordinary commitment. The crossing motif echoes the Exodus/conquest Jordan-crossings as a theological parallel.
16: wayyāḇōʾû min-bᵉnê binyāmîn wîhûḏāh — ‘and there came from the sons of Benjamin and Judah to the stronghold.’ The double reference — Benjamin (Saul’s tribe) and Judah (David’s tribe) coming together — signals the beginning of the reunification that will culminate at Hebron.
17: David’s interrogation of the arriving men: ʾim-lᵉšālôm bāʾtem ʾēlay lᵉʿāzᵉrēnî — ‘if you have come to me in peace to help me.’ But if they have come to betray him to his enemies: yōkaḥ ʾĕlōhê ʾăḇôtênû — ‘the God of our fathers will see and rebuke.’ David commends the judgment of betrayal to God rather than taking pre-emptive violence — showing the theological restraint that characterizes his fugitive period.
18: wᵉrûaḥ lāḇᵉšāh ʾet-ʿămaśay — ‘the Spirit clothed Amasai.’ The verb lāḇaš (to clothe) with Spirit-clothing is the same idiom used for Gideon (Judges 6:34) and Zechariah (2 Chronicles 24:20). The Spirit ‘wearing’ a person rather than ‘entering’ or ‘coming upon’ them is distinctive. Amasai then speaks a poetic declaration: lᵉḵā dāwid wᵉʿimmᵉḵā ben-yišay šālôm šālôm lᵉḵā — ‘Yours, David, and with you, son of Jesse! Peace, peace to you.’ The three-fold šālôm is a covenantal blessing formula.
19: Some Manassites defect to David as he goes with the Philistines to fight Saul (the battle of Jezreel/Gilboa context, 1 Sam 29). The Philistine lords dismissed David out of concern he might defect. bᵉḇôʾô ʿim-pᵉlištîm — ‘when he came with the Philistines’ — the Chronicler does not explain the awkward context (David as Philistine vassal) but includes the result: Manassites joined him.
20: The Manassite defectors named: Adnah, Jozabad, Jediael, Michael, Jozabad (second), Elihu, Zillethai — ‘heads of thousands’ (rāʾšê hāʾălāpîm) in Manasseh. These are senior military commanders changing allegiance from Saul to David, a major political shift.
21: wᵉhēmmāh ʿāzᵉrû ʾet-dāwid ʿal-haggdûḏ — ‘they helped David against the raiding band.’ The reference is to the Amalekite raid on Ziklag (1 Sam 30), where David recovered his people and plunder. kî gibbôrê ḥayil kull̄ām — ‘for they were all mighty warriors.’
22: kî lᵉʿēt yôm bᵉyôm yāḇōʾû ʿal-dāwid lᵉʿāzᵉrô ʿaḏ-lᵉmaḥăneh gāḏôl kᵉmaḥănēh ʾĕlōhîm — ‘for day by day they came to David to help him, until it was a great army like the army of God.’ The phrase maḥănēh ʾĕlōhîm (army/camp of God) appears elsewhere only in Genesis 32:2 (Jacob’s encounter with the angels). The Chronicler implicitly compares David’s growing coalition to the divine army — a remarkable theological claim about the Davidic enterprise.
23: wᵉʾēlleh misparê rāʾšê hāʾāḥûzāh lissᵉḇāʾ — ‘and these are the numbers of the armed forces.’ bāʾû ʾel-dāwid ḥeḇrônāh lᵉhāsēb malḵût šāʾûl ʾēlāyw kᵉpî YHWH — ‘to turn the kingdom of Saul to him according to the word of YHWH.’ This framing makes the Hebron anointing not a political event but the fulfillment of divine decree. The military census that follows (1 Chronicles 12:24-37) shows the scope of the all-Israel coalition.
24: Judah: 6,800 warriors bearing shield and spear. The relatively small number for Judah (compared to other tribes) may reflect that Judah had already been with David for years; the others are newly joining. The specific weapon groupings (ṭōmᵉkê ṣinnāh wārōmaḥ — shield-and-spear bearers) represent organized infantry units.
25: Simeon: 7,100 ‘mighty men of valor for the war’ (gibborê ḥayil laṣṣāḇāʾ). Simeon was geographically integrated into Judah (Joshua 19:1) and often acts as Judah’s partner (Judges 1:3). Their substantial number shows strong tribal support.
26: Levi: 4,600. Levites are counted in a military list, which is unusual — they are normally exempt from warfare (Numbers 1:47-53). The Chronicler’s inclusion of Levites in the Hebron coalition reflects his emphasis on priestly/cultic participation in establishing the Davidic order that will culminate in the temple.
27: The Aaronites specifically: Jehoiada the leader with 3,700 men. wᵉʿimmô yᵉhôyāḏāʿ hannāśîʾ lᵉʾahărōn — ‘and with him Jehoiada, prince of the house of Aaron.’ Aaron’s priestly house is thus explicitly part of the Davidic military coalition — the priestly and kingly orders are aligned from the beginning.
28: wᵉṣādôq naʿar gibbôr ḥayil ûbêt ʾāḇîw śārîm ʿeśrîm ûšnayim — ‘and Zadok, a young man mighty of valor, and his father’s house: twenty-two commanders.’ This is the first mention of Zadok in Chronicles. His presence at David’s anointing at Hebron anticipates his later appointment as chief priest (1 Chronicles 15:11; 2 Samuel 8:17). The Chronicler introduces him here to show the Zadokite priesthood’s foundation in the original Davidic covenant moment.
29: Benjamin: 3,000. But with a significant qualification: wᵉrōḇ mēhem ʿaḏ-hēnnāh šōmᵉrîm mišmeret bêt šāʾûl — ‘for the majority of them had still been keeping watch over the house of Saul.’ The small number of Benjaminites reflects the complexity of the transition — Benjamin is Saul’s tribe and many remained loyal to the Saulide house. The three thousand who came to David represent a significant but minority shift within the tribe.
30: Ephraim: 20,800, described as gibborê ḥayil anšê šēmōt lᵉbêt ʾăḇôtām — ‘mighty men of valor, famous men in their fathers’ houses.’ The phrase anšê šēmōt (‘men of names/fame’) is unusual — these are warriors of established reputation, not anonymous troops.
31: Half-tribe of Manasseh (west): 18,000, specifically named by name (niqḇû bišmôt) — a special notation suggesting these were individually identified in the administrative record. Their 18,000 represents substantial Manassite commitment to the Davidic transition.
32: ûmibᵉnê yiśśāḵār yôḏᵉʿê bîn laʿittîm laḏaʿat mah-yāʿăśeh yiśrāʾēl — ‘and from the sons of Issachar, those who understood the times, to know what Israel should do.’ This famous phrase (yôḏᵉʿê bîn laʿittîm) attributes to Issachar a quality of political-theological wisdom — understanding the ‘times’ (ʿittîm) means discerning the moment when David’s kingship was the proper order of things. kol-ʾăḥêhem ʿal-pîhem — ‘and all their brothers were under their command’ — Issachar’s wisdom leaders guide the whole tribe.
33: Zebulun: 50,000 warriors, specifically commended as lōʾ bᵉlēḇ wālēḇ — ‘not of double heart’ (literally ‘not with heart and heart’). The double heart (lēḇ wālēḇ) signifies divided allegiance (cf. Psalms 12:2). Zebulun’s complete commitment is distinguished from Benjamin’s divided loyalty (1 Chronicles 12:29). The large number and the commendation make Zebulun the paradigm warrior-tribe at Hebron.
34: Naphtali: 1,000 commanders, 37,000 shield-and-spear warriors. The large base force with named commanders suggests Naphtali had a well-organized military structure. The north was strongly Davidic here.
35: Dan: 28,600 armed for war. Dan’s territory was in the far north (following their migration in Judges 18), making their participation a statement of all-Israel support from the most geographically remote southern tribe’s counterpart.
36: Asher: 40,000 warriors fit for war (bᵉʿōrᵉkê milḥāmāh — arranged/ordered for war). Asher, another northern coastal tribe, contributes substantially. The numbers mount as the list moves north, reaching a crescendo of pan-Israel military commitment.
37: Trans-Jordanian contingent: Reuben, Gad, and half-tribe of Manasseh (east): 120,000 with all manner of weapons of war (kol-kᵉlê milḥāmāh). This is the largest single block count. The trans-Jordanian tribes — geographically separated from the west by the Jordan — are fully committed. Their inclusion fulfills the geographic scope of the Davidic kingdom (‘all Israel’).
38: kol-ʾēlleh anšê milḥāmāh ʿōzrê maʿărāḵāh bᵉlēḇāḇ šālēm bāʾû ḥeḇrônāh lᵉhamlîḵ ʾet-dāwid — ‘all these men of war, keeping ranks, came to Hebron with a whole heart to make David king over all Israel.’ The phrase lēḇāḇ šālēm (whole/undivided heart) is the Chronicler’s signature expression for the proper covenantal posture (cf. 1 Chronicles 28:9; 1 Chronicles 29:19; 2 Chronicles 15:17; 1 Chronicles 16:9). wᵉgam kol-šᵉʾērît yiśrāʾēl lēḇ ʾeḥāḏ — ‘and also all the rest of Israel was of one heart to make David king.’ The one-heart formula is the Chronicler’s ideal of national unity.
39: wayyihyû-šām ʿim-dāwid šᵉlōšet yāmîm ʾōḵlîm wᵉšōtîm — ‘they were there with David three days, eating and drinking.’ The three-day covenant feast at Hebron echoes covenant meal patterns (Genesis 31:54; Exodus 24:11). Shared eating seals the covenant of kingship. The Chronicler’s interest in communal celebration here anticipates the great liturgical celebrations of 1 Chronicles 15-16 and Solomon’s temple dedication.
40: wᵉgam qᵉrôḇêhem ʿaḏ-yiśśāḵār wᵉzᵉḇûlun wᵉnaptālî mᵉḇîʾîm leḥem ʿal-ḥămōrîm — ‘and also those nearest to them, as far as Issachar and Zebulun and Naphtali, brought bread on donkeys.’ The list of provisions — bread, figs, raisins, wine, oil, oxen, sheep — represents a banquet of all-Israel abundance. The provision-list functions as a symbol of national solidarity: every major food item is contributed, from grain products to livestock to wine. The feast is not just political but a covenant celebration in the fullest sense.
